coredata.dc:description: Rare earth upconversion nanoparticle and graphene quantum dot have attracted a great research interest in biomedicine, but they all have some inherent defects as nanocarriers for in vivo drug delivery system. The study reported one new concept for fabrication of graphene quantum dot-rare earth upconversion hybrid via coordination between rare earth ion and histidine and octadecylamine-functionalized graphene quantum dot and subsequent fluoride ion replacement and hydrothermal treatment. The as-synthesized hybrid exhibits cage-like nanostructure with the mean particle diameter of 46.2 nm comprising of several nanometers of graphene sheets and NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocrystals, high stability in a physiological buffer and 140.2-fold enhancement of upconversion luminescence. The biomimetic drug delivery system, composing of the hybrid and gold nanoparticles as the core and MGC-803 cell membranes as the shell, realizes the doxorubicin (DOX) loading capacity of 461.2%, targeting of homotypic tumor cells, both pH and light-stimulated drug release and chemo/photothermal therapy with a higher anticanceractivity compared with free DOX. The therapeutic evaluation proves excellent anticancer activity and biocompatibility. The study also open one way to design of ideal nanocarriers for cancer diagnosis and treatment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of MGC-803 cell membrane-coated His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocages for pH and light-stimulated DOX release and chemo/photothermal therapy of cancer.
Fabrication process (A), TEM (B and C), HRTEM images (D) and HAADF-STEM images (E) and elemental mappings of Y (F), Yb (G), F(H), Tm (I) and N (J) of His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocages.
The fluorescence emission spectrum (A) of His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm under 980 nm laser excitation and energy level diagram (B) of NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocrystals.

The fluorescence emission spectra (A) of GYAu nanoconjugates with the Au/GY mass ratio of 0.0, 0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.05, 0.075, 0.10, 0.125, 0.15, 0.175, 0.2 and 0.25 (from back to front) under 980 nm laser excitation with power density of 0.5 W cm−2, the time-dependent temperature profiles (B) of GYAu, GY, Au, and buffer (0.2 M NaCl, 10 mM Tris-HCl) under 980 nm laser radiation with the power density of 0.5 W cm−2, the fluorescence spectra (C) of DOX before and after added GY, the thermodynamic parameters (D) of ΔH, -TΔS, and ΔG for the combination process of GYAu with DOX, the time-dependent DOX loading content profile (E) of CGYAu-DOX system and the time-dependent DOX release rates (F) of CGYAu-DOX system at different pH values with and without a 980 nm laser radiation at the power density of 0.5 W cm−2.

The confocal images (A) of MGC-803 cells incubated in CGYAu, free DOX, CGYAu-DOX or CGYAu-DOX with NIR laser radiation for 4 h, in which the nucleus was stained with Hoechst 33342, and the intracellular DOX fluorescence intensity (B) of MGC-803 cells incubated in different nanoconjugates for 1, 2, 3 and 4 h determined by flow cytometry.

In vitro dark cytotoxicity (A) of CGYAu, DOX, GYAu-DOX and CGYAu-DOX and the phototoxicity (B) of DOX, GYAu-DOX and CGYAu-DOX under the NIR laser irradiation of DOX at different concentrations on GMC-803 cells after 48 h incubation.
Quantitative in vivo bio-distribution analysis of DOX in different formulations at 1, 4 and 24 h after intravenous injection in the nude mouse bearing MGC-803 tumors.

The digital photographs (A) of tumor-bearing mouse taken after treatments, MGC-803 tumor growth curves with various treatments (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01) (B), and histological images (C) of major organs collected from the tumor-bearing mouse after treated with saline, DOX, CGYAu, GYAu-DOX, CGYAu-DOX and CGYAu-DOX + NIR.
Time-dependent distribution of CGYAu-DOX at various organs (a) and excretion (b) by measuring Y concentration (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01).

The nude mice bearing MGC-803 tumors after injection of saline (control) and GY for 1 day and 7 days. The blood cell counts, kidney and liver function markers including BUN, CR, ALT and AST were detected.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot-rare earth upconversion nanocages with extremely high efficiency of upconversion luminescence, stability and drug loading towards controlled delivery and cancer theranostics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study reported one concept for fabrication of functional graphene quantum dot-rare earth upconversion nanocage. The nanocage exhibits extremely high efficiency of upconversion luminescence, stability and drug loading toward controlled delivery and cancer theranostics.

Rare earth upconversion nanoparticle and graphene quantum dot have attracted a great research interest in biomedicine, but they all have some inherent defects as nanocarriers for in vivo drug delivery system. The study reported one new concept for fabrication of graphene quantum dot-rare earth upconversion hybrid via coordination between rare earth ion and histidine and octadecylamine-functionalized graphene quantum dot and subsequent fluoride ion replacement and hydrothermal treatment. The as-synthesized hybrid exhibits cage-like nanostructure with the mean particle diameter of 46.2 nm comprising of several nanometers of graphene sheets and NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocrystals, high stability in a physiological buffer and 140.2-fold enhancement of upconversion luminescence. The biomimetic drug delivery system, composing of the hybrid and gold nanoparticles as the core and MGC-803 cell membranes as the shell, realizes the doxorubicin (DOX) loading capacity of 461.2%, targeting of homotypic tumor cells, both pH and light-stimulated drug release and chemo/photothermal therapy with a higher anticanceractivity compared with free DOX. The therapeutic evaluation proves excellent anticancer activity and biocompatibility. The study also open one way to design of ideal nanocarriers for cancer diagnosis and treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a serious threat to human health that causes millions of deaths every year [1]. Chemotherapy is the most commonly used option for cancer treatment after the operation. However, chemotherapy often fails to cancer treatment and prolongs patient survival due to its severe toxicity to normal cells and side effects. To improve the efficacy, various drug delivery systems with different nanocarriers have been established as multifunctional platforms for diagnosis and treatment of tumors [2–7]. These nanocarriers tend to accumulate in cancer lesions through leaking cancer vessels for enhancing permeability and retention effect [8]. Rare earth upconversion nanoparticle (UCNP) [9–13] and graphene quantum dot (GQD) [14] received a more attention in drug delivery systems due to their unique structures and properties.

UCNP offers some magical optical properties, including large Stokes shift, low toxicity, high photo-stability and negligible background fluorescence [15,16]. By one reasonable design, UCNP can produce a full spectrum upconversion fluorescence emission from ultraviolet light to near infrared light under near infrared radiation. The emission light may synchronously meet multiple needs of drug delivery and cancer therapy. The ultraviolet light has high energy and can be used as the light source for photodynamic therapy of cancer. The visible light gives a low energy and little damage to normal cells and can be used as the light source for light-controlled drug release, which requires long-term light radiation during the tumor treatment. The near infrared light gives a much stronger penetrating ability than visible light and ultraviolet light. It can be used for deep tissue imaging in early diagnosis and visualization of cancer treatment. These remarkable characteristics determine that UCNP is one of the best optical probe for in vivo drug delivery systems. However, biomedical applications of UCNP suffer from complex synthetic process, low efficiency of upconversion luminescence, poor dispersibility in a physiological buffer and low drug loading capacity. To resolve the problems, many functional materials have applied in modifying UCNP such as polymers [15,17,18], porous silicon [19], biomolecule [20], protein [21] and hairpin DNA [22]. However, fabrication of ideal UCNP-based nanocarriers for drug delivery and cancer treatment with high efficiency of upconversion luminescence, stability and drug loading capacity still faces great challenge up to now [23–26].

GQD is a promising graphene material with size of several nanometers, strong quantum confinement and edge effect [27]. Compared with classical graphene and graphite oxide, GQD offers a much larger surface area, higher water-solubility, more functional groups and better bioavailability. The investigations have demonstrated that GQD can strongly bind to some anticancerdrugs such as doxorubicin (DOX) via Van der Waals force and hydrogen bond in the neutral condition of pH 7 and release the DOX in an acid condition of pH 5 [28,29]. The release mechanism is favorable to anticancer drug delivery for reducing the toxicity to normal cells, because tumor cells always provides a more acidic intracellular environment than normal cells [30]. More interestingly, the formed GQD-DOX nanoconjugate often gives a much higher nuclear uptake of DOX than that of free DOX. This leads to the improvement of chemotherapeutic efficacy [31]. In addition, GQD facilitates targeting delivery of anticancer drugs via the functionalization with targeting molecules [32]. These prominent characteristics determine that GQD is one of the best nanocarriers in the drug delivery of cancer. By one reasonable design, GQD can realize high loading of anticancer drugs, stability in a physiological buffer condition and cancer targeting. However, the fluorescence emission of GQD is not suitable as the light source for in vivo imaging and light-controlled drug release [33–35]. Mostly, GQD emits one blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light radiation. The short-wavelength fluorescence only offers a weak penetration ability, which cannot reach the deep tissues. Moreover, long-term ultraviolet radiation also may cause a serious damage to normal cells due to its high energy.

UCNP and GQD have become “star materials” in the field of nanomedicine, but they all have some inherent defects as the nanocarriers for in vivo drug delivery systems that come from their characteristic structures and properties. The hybrid of UCNP and GQD may pave one way to realize their complementary advantages and significant synergy. In the study, histidine and octadecylamine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (His/OA-GQD) was designed, synthesized and used for fabrication of His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocages. The as-synthesized His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm exhibits extremely high efficiency of upconversion luminescence, stability in a physiological buffer and DOX drug loading. The drug delivery system (Fig. 1 ), composing of the nanocages and gold nanoparticle as the core and MGC-803 cell membrane as the shell, realizes excellent biocompatibility, selective targeting of homotypic tumor cells, both pH and light-stimulated DOX release and chemo/photothermal therapy with a much higher anticanceractivity compared with free DOX.

Yttrium chloride (YCl3), ytterbium chloride (YbCl3), thulium chloride (TmCl3), sodium fluoride (NaF), citric acid (CA), histidine (His), octadecylamine (OA), gold chloride (HAuCl4) and tetrakis-hydroxymethyl-phosphonium chloride, doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX), SRB and Hoechst 33,342 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). Soybean phosphatidylcholine (S100, SPC) was obtained from Lipoid GmbH (Ludwigshafen, Germany). Protease Inhibitor Cocktail (EDTA-Free, 100 in dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO)) was purchased from SelleckChem (Houston, USA). Trypan Blueand bicinch onininc acid (BCA) protein assay kit was obtained from Beyotime (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM), penicillin-streptomycin (10,000 U/mL), phosphate buffered saline (PBS), Tirs-HCl buffer (0.2 M NaCl, 10 mM tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane, pH 7) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Gibco. Kits for CellTiter-Blue Cell Viability Assay were obtained from PromegaCorporation. All other reagents employed were of analytical reagent grade or with the highest quality and were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). The ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) purified from a Milli-Q purification system was used throughout the experiment.

The material morphology and structure were characterized by FESEM (GeminiSEM 500), TEM (JEOL, JEM-1011), HRTEM, EELS maps and HAADF-STEM images (JEOL JEM-ARM 200F field-emission atomic-resolution analytical microscope). The surface compositions of materials were investigated by XPS (PHI 5700 ESCA) using Al Kα (hν = 1486.6 eV) X-ray radiation, and the binding energies were calibrated using C1s peak (284.8 eV) as the reference. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on Mettler Toledo TGA/SDTA851 thermal analyzer. Infrared spectra (IR) were recorded on Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) image was obtained on Multimode 8 force microscope (Bruker, Germany). Downconversion fluorescence spectrum and intensity were recorded on Cary Esclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, Japan) with the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The UV–Visible absorption spectra were measured on Shimadzu UV-2550 UV/visible spectrophotometer. Upconversion photoluminescent spectra were measured using Cary Esclipse spectrofluorometer, in conjunction with 980 nm diode laser with tunable output power from 0 to 2 W (Changchun New Industries Optoelectronics Technology Co., Ltd., China). Upconversion luminescence microscopy was performed on Olympus BX51 microscope with a xenon lamp adapted to 980 nm diode laser. Animal studies were conducted using a Leica TCS SP8 (Leica Microsystems, Germany) in vivo imaging system. UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on TU-1901 spectrophotometer (Beijing Persee General instrument Co., Ltd., China), and NIR spectra were collected with UV-3600 plus UV–VIS-NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

His/OA-GQD was prepared by one-step thermal pyrolysis of the mixture citric acid, histidine and octadecylamine. In a typical procedure, citric acid (0.1 mol) was mixed with histidine (0.12 mol) and octadecylamine (0.001 mol). Followed by heating at 180 °C for 5 h and cooling to room temperature to obtain His/OA-GQD crude. The crude was dissolved in ultra pure water to form transparent His/OA-GQD solution (50 mg mL−1). The solution was dialyzed in dialysis bag with 3 kDa cut-off molecular weight with change of water every 6 h before the precipitate occurred in the bag. The solution in the bag was dried by free-drying to obtain a solid His/OA-GQD product.

YCl3 (15 mmol), YbCl3 (4.0 mmol) and TmCl3 (1.0 mmol) are dissolved in ultrapure water (15 mL). Then, drops the His/OA-GQD aqueous solution (50 mg mL−1, 150 mL). The precipitation was collected, washed with ultrapure water and re-dispersed in ultrapure water (20 mL). Adds the NaF aqueous solution (1.0 M, 240 mL) and then continues to stir for 60 min. The obtained dispersion was transferred into a pressure reactor, heated at 180 °C for 5 h, cools to room temperature and collects the supernatant in sequence. The solution is given a centrifuge separation at 11500 rpm for 20 min and washed with ultrapure water for 3 times to remove free His/OA-GQD. The as-synthesized His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm was re-dispersed in ultrapure water with the concentration of 2.0 mg mL−1 and stored in refrigerator at 4 °C before use.

The DOX solution was added to the His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm solution and incubated with stirring overnight at room temperature. The formed GY-DOX nanoconjugate was centrifuged for 15 min at 12000 rpm and washed with the buffer solution (0.2 M NaCl, 10 mM Tris-HCl) to remove non-specifically bonded DOX molecules and then re-dispersed in the buffer solution (0.2 M NaCl, 10 mM Tris-HCl). To obtain GYAu-DOX nanoconjugate, the GY-DOX was mixed with a freshly prepared Au NPs for 15 min while undergoing ultrasonic irradiation. Subsequently, the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 6 h. The resulted GYAu-DOX was isolated by centrifugation, washed with H2O for several times and re-dispersed in H2O.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MGC-803 cell membrane vesicles@His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm/DOX/AuNPs nanoconjugate (CGYAu-DOX) preparation
ce:section.ce:para: MGC-803 cell membrane vesicles (CMVs) as the outer shells were prepared. Typically, the harvested MGC-803 cells were treated with the hypertonic Tris-HCI buffer (pH 7.4) at 4 °C for 1 h, homogenized at 22000 rpm for 1 min (Auto HG-24, XinRui Technology, China) and centrifuged at 500×g for 10 min to remove the intracellular contents. The supernatants were centrifuged at 10000×g for 10 min and 100000×g for 1 h (BiofugeStratos, Thermo) to get the cell pellets. The pellets were washed with PBS. The resulting pellets were sonicated for 5 s using an ultrasonicator (JYD-650L, Zhixin Inc., China) and extruded through 400 nm and subsequent 200 nm polycarbonate membranes (LiposoFast LF1, Avestin, Canada). The obtained CMVs were quantified by lyophilization and extruded through 100 nm polycarbonate membranes. Then, the CMVs (1.2 mg) were well mixed with the as-prepared GYAu-DOX to obtain CGYAu-DOX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DOX loading content (LC) and encapsulation efficiency (EE) measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
The concentration of DOX in the initial drug solution and supernatant was measured by using spectrophotometer based on the absorbance at 480 nm. The LC and EE of DOX were calculated according to the following Eqs. (1) and (2):
(1)
LC =
m 0
-
m f
/
m c
× 100 %
(2)
EE =
m o
-
m f
mo × 100 %
where mo is total mass of DOX added, mf is the DOX mass in the supernatant after the centrifugation, and mc is the mass of nanocarriers added in the loading process.

The sample solution (1.0 mL, 10 μg mL−1) (GYAu, GY, Au or Tris-HCl buffer) was added into a transparent quartz vial. Then, the bottom of vial was radiated by a fiber-coupled semiconductor diode NIR laser (980 nm) with a power density of 0.5 W cm−2. Thermal profiles of different materials were measured upon radiation of 980 nm laser (Changchun New Industries, China) at 0.5 W cm−2, the thermographic images were determined by an infrared thermal camera (A150-15-M, Irtech Ltd.), and the temperatures at different time points were recorded by the accompanied software.

To determine the pH and photo-stimulated DOX release, the CGYAu-DOX solution (10 μg mL−1) was divided into six groups: a) pH 5.0 in dark, b) pH 6.0 in dark, c) pH 7.4 in dark, and d) pH 5.0, e) pH 6.0 and f) pH 7.4 with NIR laser radiation (980 nm, 0.5 W cm−2) at selected time points. After that, the solution was treated by centrifugation at 14500 rpm and the supernatant was collected. The released amount of DOX was determined according to the absorbance at 480 nm of DOX in the supernatant.

The cytotoxicity was measured using CellTiter-Blue®cell viability assay on MGC-803 human gastric cancer cells according to the manufacturer's instructions. The cells are seeded into a 96-well plate at 5 × 103/well and cultured at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h. Add 10 μL of the nanoconjugate aqueous solution with different concentrations to the wells and then incubate with the cells for 24 h at 37 °C under 5% CO2. CellTiter-Blue reagent (20 μL) was added to the each well and then incubated with the cells for 1–4 h (37 °C, 5% CO2). The plate was shaken for 10 s and fluorescence was measured on the micro plate reader at 560/590 nm (ex/em). The inhibition of cell growth was determined by following formula (3):
(3)
Cell viability
( % )
=
mean fluorescence value of treatment group
/
mean fluorescence value of control
× 100 %

To test the intracellular uptake of DOX-loaded formulations, the MGC-803 cells seeded in the 96-well plates were treated with the free DOX, CGYAu, CGYAu-DOX and CGYAu-DOX with the NIR radiation of 5 min for 4 h at the equivalent DOX concentration of 10 μg mL−1, respectively. Then, the cells were stained with Hoechst 33342, washed with PBS, and imaged using a fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan). To further quantitatively determine the cellular internalization, the MGC-803 cells treated with the free DOX, GYAu-DOX, CGYAu-DOX and CGYAu-DOX with NIR laser radiation of 5 min were incubated for 1, 2, and 4 h at the equivalent DOX concentration of 10 μg mL−1, respectively. Afterward, the cells were collected, washed, and suspended in PBS to detect the fluorescence intensity of DOX using a flow cytometry, and the data were analyzed with a Cell Quest software.

MGC-803 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well and incubated for 48 h. Then, the cells were treated with DOX, GYAu-DOX and CGYAu-DOX at DOX concentrations from 0.01 to 10 μg mL−1 or CGYAu with the concentrations from 0.10 to 200 μg mL−1 for 24 h. The plates used to investigate the dark toxicity of the formulations were kept on incubation in the dark for another 48 h. The other treated plates with the same formulations prepared to investigate the phototoxicity were exposed to 980 nm laser radiation at 1.0 W cm−2 for 5 min and incubated for another 24 h. The SRB assay was applied to measure cell viability.

Therapeutics efficacy of NIR-mediated drug release for MGC-803 cancer cells was tested by a calcein-AM/propidium iodide (PI) staining technique. In a typical procedure, MGC-803 cells are incubated with the DOX-loaded nanoconjugates (1.0 mg) for 6 h at 37 °C. Followed the dissociation with trypsin, centrifugation at 300g for 2 min and washing with PBS in sequence. The collected cells are incubated in a cell culture incubator (5%CO2) with the PBS solution containing Calcein-AM and PI for 20 min at 37 °C. The resulting samples are used for viability flow cytometry assessment on a FACSCalibur flow cytometry instrument (Becton, Dickinson and Company, America). The obtained fluorescence signals reflect the living status of cells. The Calcein-AM emission at 515 nm was obtained with the 490-nm excitation and PI at 617 nm was detected under the 535-nm excitation.

MGC-803 tumor bearing Balb/c nude mices were first anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of ketamine. The tumor-bearing mice were randomized into four groups (N = 6, each group). For the therapy group, tumor-bearing mice are injected with the DOX-loaded nanoconjugates at the tumor site and then irradiated with a 980 nm laser (1.0 W/cm2 for 5 min, 1 min interval after 1 min irradiation). The tumor size was measured using one Vernier caliper every 2 days. The tumor volume (V) is calculated according to the formula V = L × W/2, where L and W are the length and width of the tumor, respectively.

Male Balb/c mouse (4–5 week old, 18–20 g) were purchased from Shanghai Experimental Animal Center (Shanghai). The mouse were randomly divided into two groups including blank group and GY treated group. For GY treated group, mouse were intravenously injected with 200 μL of GY (10 mg kg−1), and then the blood was collected for subsequent analysis after post injection for 1 day and 7 days. The blood cell counts were measured on an automated hematology analyzer (Bayer Advia 2120). The serum biochemistry parameters including blood urea nitrogen (BUN), creatinine (CR), aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) were tested using the automatic biochemical analyzer (Olympus AU400).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo near infrared fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The tumor bearing mouse were randomly divided into three groups after the tumor size reaches to about 200 mm3: saline, DOX (200 μg), and CGYAu-DOX (DOX: 200 μg), with an intravenous injection of 200 μL of drug via tails vein. In vivo imaging system equipped with a 980 nm optical fiber-coupled laser was employed for image the mice. The laser power density was about 0.5 W/cm2 during imaging with an exposure time of 30 s. A 790/40 nm band-pass emission filter was applied to prevent the interference of excitation light to the CCD camera.

The in vivo biodistribution assay was determined in the orthotopic gastric tumor model. The female nude mouse were subcutaneously injected with 100 μL of cell suspension (1 × 106 MGC-803 cells) to initiate the gastric tumor. After two weeks, the nude mouse were randomly assigned to three groups and intravenously injected with DOX, CGYAu-DOX and CGYAu-DOX with NIR irradiation of 5 min receiving injection for 1 h, respectively (5 mg kg−1 DOX). For each group, the mouse were sacrificed at 1, 4 and 24 h after administration. The main organs including tumor, kidney, lung, spleen, liver and heart were collected and washed with PBS. Then the typical organs were weighed and about 100 mg of each organ was homogenized in DMSO, followed by centrifugation at 20,000×g for 10 min. The collected supernatants were measured for the DOX concentration by fluorescence spectrophotometry and expressed as DOX weight in per gram of tissue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antitumor efficiency and biosafety
ce:section.ce:para: The tumor model was established by subcutaneously injecting 100 μL of PBS containing 1 × 106 MGC-803 cells into the hind flank of male Balb/c mouse. After the tumor size reached about 200 mm3 (set as Day 0), the mouse bearing-MGC-803 tumors were randomly divided into six groups (6 animals per group) and treated with various samples (150 μL): saline, CGYAu (2.5 mg kg−1 body weight), DOX (2.5 mg kg−1 body weight), CGYAu + NIR (980 nm, 1.0 W cm−2, 3 min. CGYAu: 2.5 mg kg−1 body weight), CGYAu-DOX (DOX: 2.5 mg kg−1 body weight), CGYAu-DOX + NIR (980 nm, 1.0 W cm−2, 3 min. DOX: 2.5 mg kg−1 body weight). According to the results of in vivo near infrared fluorescence imaging, the mouse were irradiated at 8 h after the intravenous injection. The tumor size was monitored every other day by a caliper and the volume (V) was calculated as: V = 0.5 × L × W2, in which L and W are long diameter and short diameter respectively. At the same time, the body weight was recorded. The mouse were euthanized after 14 days of treatment, and the main tissues including hearts, livers, spleens, lungs, kidneys and tumors were harvested and treated with formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded and H&E stained for further histological examination.

The tumor-bearing Balb/c nude mouse in the therapy and control groups were anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of ketamine (150 mg kg−1)/xylazine (10 mg kg−1). The tumors and selected organs were isolated from the mouse and stored in tissue freezing medium. The resulted mixture was slowly cooled down in dry ice chamber to form a white solid. The obtained samples were placed overnight at −80 °C. Then, the tissues were cut to 10-μM thick sections and collected with glass slide by using cryostat instrument after the temperature of tissues was increased to −20 °C. In a typical experiment, the tissue slides were washed with the PBS buffer (3 × 2 min), fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA) for 15 min and then washed with PBS buffer (3 × 4 min). Next, the tissue slides are incubated with Hematoxylin for 5 min and gently washed with ultrapure water for at least 10 min to stain the nuclei. Then, the eosin solution (1%) is gently added dropwise to cover the whole tissue. After 3–5 min, the tissues were washed with ultrapure water to remove the unstained eosin. After that, the tissues on the glass slides are dehydrated using alcohol and xylene solution. The final glass slides are mounted by using Canada balsam mounting solution and the images are taken in a Zeiss microscope with color camera.

Histidine and octadecylamine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (His/OA-GQD) was designed and synthesized in the concept. Here, citric acid (CA) was used as the carbon source for making graphene sheets. Owing to special molecule structure, citric acid is easy to be converted into graphene sheets by intermolecular condensation and subsequent carbonization [36–38]. Histidine (His) was used for introducing imidazole groups into edge of graphene sheets, which can strongly coordinate with rare earth ion (RE3+) to form stable RE complex. The imidazole groups in His/OA-GQD plays important roles in the fabrication of His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocage. The first role is to adjust the size of NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocrystals. Because of strong coordination of His/OA-GQD with RE3+, the release of RE3+ from the RE complex is a slow process. This remarkably decreases the rate for formation and crystal growth of NaYF4:Yb,Tm, finally leading to producing small NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanoparticles. The second role is to realize the fixation of His/OA-GQD on the surface of NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocrystals. With the formation of hybrid, His/OA-GQD as the stabilizing agent is covalently fixed to the surface of NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanoparticles. The connection helps to achieves ultrafast electron/energy transfer process between His/OA-GQD and NaYF4:Yb,Tm, improving the optical and electrical properties. The third role is to enhance the drug loading capacity. As the imidazole group can bind to anticancer drug such as DOX via the π-π stacking to form the nanoconjugate, its introduction enhances the drug loading capacity due to more binding sites between GQD and DOX. Octadecylamine (OA) was used for introducing hydrophobic alkyl chain into edge of graphene sheets to construct more reasonable amphiphilic structure. The amphiphilic structure makes the hybrid good surface activity. This makes it easier to enter into the interior of cancer cells via lipid-soluble cell membrane of cancer cells. The as-designed His/OA-GQD was prepared by one-step simple pyrolysis of the mixed CA with His and OA. The reaction conditions were optimized, including molar ratio of His/CA, molar ratio of OA/CA, reaction time and reaction temperature. The best reaction conditions were found to be His/CA of 1.2, OA/CA of 0.01, reaction time of 5 h and temperature of 180 °C (Synthesis of His/OA-GQD in Supporting Information, Fig. S1–7). The resulted His/OA-GQD was characterized by TEM, selected area electron diffraction patterns, AFM, IR spectrum and XPS technologies and its physicochemical properties were investigated, including optical property, surface activity and interaction with DOX. The results indicate that His/OA-GQD is composed of the graphene sheets with the mean particle size of 3.4 nm, contains the rich of hydrophilic and functional groups, and offers high fluorescence emission, good surface activity and strong interaction with DOX (Structure characterization of His/OA-GQD and Physicochemical properties of His/OA-GQD in Supporting Information, Fig. S8–12 and Table S1).

The as-prepared His/OA-GQD was used for fabrication of His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm hybrid. Firstly, His/OA-GQD was reacted with the rare earth (RE) ions (Y3+, Yb3+ and Tm3+) to form the corresponding RE complex. Then, His/OA-GQD in the RE complex is partly replaced by F- to produce the NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocrystals via the addition of NaF and subsequent hydrothermal treatment. The above synthesis principle has been confirmed by monitoring fluorescence change of the reaction system (Synthesis principle in Supporting Information, Fig. S13). The study reveals that the GQD plays key roles in adjusting size, controlling morphology and surface modification of NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocrystals. Interestingly, these roles seriously depend on the structure, property and size of graphene sheets of the GQD (Roles of His/OA-GQD in Supporting Information, Fig. S14 and 15). The as-synthesized was characterized and the results were shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. S16. It can be seen from Fig. 2A–D that the hybrid offers a cage-like nanostructure with the mean particle diameter of 46.2 nm, comprising of several nanometers of graphene sheets and NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocrystals, and high crystallinity. The distance between the lattice fringes were found to be 0.22 nm and 0.18 nm, corresponding to the (1 0 0) and (1 1 0) spacing of hexagonal NaYF4 crystals (Insert in Fig. 2D), respectively. The atomic composition of His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocages was verified from the results of HAADF-STEM and elemental mapping of Y, Yb, F, Tm and N (Fig. 2F–J). As Y, Yb, Tm and F elements only exist in the NaYF4:Yb,Tm and N element only exists in the His/OA-GQD, the above results also demonstrate that our synthesis achieves to the hybrid of NaYF4:Yb,Tm and His/OA-GQD. There are fifteen diffraction peaks at 17.2°, 30.1°, 30.8°, 34.8°, 39.7°, 43.5°, 46.6°, 52.0°, 53.3°, 53.7°, 55.2°, 61.1°, 62.4°, 63.7° and 65.2° on the XRD patterns on the XRD pattern in Fig. S16A. These peaks are corresponded to the (1 0 0), (1 1 0), (1 0 1), (2 0 0), (1 1 1), (2 0 1), (2 1 0), (0 0 2), (3 0 0), (2 1 1), (1 0 2), (1 1 2), (2 2 0), (2 0 2) and (3 1 0) crystal planes of hexagonal β-NaYF4 nanocrystals (PDF card No. 16–0334), respectively. Owing to the poor crystallinity of His/OA-GQD, the characteristic peak at 26° of graphene is so weak that it cannot be found in Fig. S16A. There are many IR absorption peaks and absorption bands on the IR spectrum in Fig. S16B. The absorption band in the wavenumber range of 3600–3000 cm−1 is the IR absorption caused by symmetric stretching vibration of N–H in –NH2 and O–H bonds. The absorption band between 3000 and 2800 cm−1 is attributed to symmetric stretching vibration of the saturated C–H bond. The peak at 1690 cm−1 is assigned to stretch vibration IR absorption of the CO bond. The peak at 1570 cm−1 is IR absorption of the –CN bond stretching vibration. The peak at 1380 cm−1 is due to stretching vibration of -C–H bond. The peak at 1150 cm−1 is attributed to stretching vibrations of C-O bond. As NaYF4 has no obvious IR absorption between 3850 and 550 cm−1, the peaks and bands in Fig. S16B are due to the IR absorption produced by His/OA-GQD in the nanocages. Further, the His/OA-GQD content in the hybrid was determined by thermogravimetric analysis (Fig. S17). The result in shows that the His/OA-GQD content was 47.4%.

The optical property of His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocages was evaluated and the results were shown in Fig. 3 . The hybrid produces a strong upconversion luminescence under 980 nm laser radiation. The emission is a typical spectrum of upconversion luminscence from Yb and Tm-doped NaYF4 crystals. The fact verifies that the hybrid with His/OA-GQD doesn’t influence on the energy levels of NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocrystals. The fluorescence spectrum includes two ultraviolet emission peaks at 350 nm and 360 nm, owing to 1D2 → 3H6 and 0P3 → 3H6 transitions, two blue emission peaks at 450 and 475 nm, owing to 1D2 → 3F4 and 1G4 → 3H6 transitions, one red emission peak at 647 nm, corresponding to 3F2 → 3H6 transition [39], and two near infrared emission peak at 780 and 800 nm, owing to 1G4 → 3H5 and 3H4 → 3H6 transitions [40]. The upconversion fluorescence quantum yield reaches to 5.72%, which is more than 140.2-fold that of NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocrystals prepared in the absence of His/OA-GQD. The result confirms that the introduction of His/OA-GQD creates a significant enhancement of upconversion luminescence. The study also shows that the enhancement depends on the downconversion fluorescence intensity of His/OA-GQD and its absorption ability towards the near infrared excitation light. For synthesis of His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocages, the His/OA-GQD prepared by using longer reaction time always brings a higher efficiency of upconversion luminescence when the reaction time is lower than 5 h. The study further proves that the enhancement is mainly attributed to the effective fluorescence resonance energy transfer between His/OA-GQD and NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocrystals as well as the absorption of His/OA-GQD as the antenna towards the near infrared excitation light (Optical property of His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm in Supporting Information, Fig. S18–20).

The biomimetic drug delivery system, composing of His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm (termed as GY) and gold nanoparticles (termed as Au) as the core and MGC-803 cell membranes as the shell (termed as C), was designed and prepared by using the reported methods [41]. The resulting CGYAu nanoconjugates were employed as the drug delivery system for drug delivery and theranostics of cancer.

The photoluminescence properties of GYAu nanoconjugates as the function of Au content was examined. The emission intensity of GYAu nanoconjugates in both ultraviolet range and visible range displays a decreasing with the increase of Au/GY mass ratio, but the fluorescence intensity at about 800 nm almost remains unchanged (Fig. 4 A). Only the near infrared emission peak at about 800 nm can be observed when the Au/GY is more than 0.15. This is because the Au nanoparticles can absorb the ultraviolet light and visible light to produce heat. The photothermal effects of different materials under the NIR light radiation were shown in Fig. 4B. The temperature of GYAu in the Tris-HCI buffer condition (pH 5.0) increased by 15.8 °C within 25 min, while the increases in temperatures for GY, the Au and Buffer systems are 6.0, 4.9 and 4.1 °C, respectively. These data prove that the GYAu gives a faster and higher increase in the temperature compared with the buffer, Au and GY systems. The previous study has demonstrated that the GY can offer a relatively high efficiency of upconversion luminescence. The produced ultraviolet light and visible light can be effectively absorbed by Au to produce more heat, which brings a faster increase in temperature. The study also confirms that the increase in temperature of GYAu highly depends on the power of NIR radiation. The temperature will rapidly increase with the increase of power density. The excellent photothermal performance of GYAu helps to improve the near infrared light-induced drug release and photothermal therapy of tumors (Loading and release behaviors of DOX from His-GQD-HAD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm in Supporting Information, Fig. S21–23).

The interaction between GY and DOX was studied by monitoring the change in fluorescence intensity of DOX before and after added GY. The fluorescence intensity remarkably reduces after added the GY (Fig. 4C), verifying the existence of strong interaction between DOX and GY. The His/OA-GQD in GY contains small graphene sheets, hydrophobic long alkyl chain and hydrophilic groups such as –OH, the GY. Thus, GY can bind to DOX via the π-π stacking, hydrophobic interaction and hydrogen bond to form stable GY-DOX nanoconjugates. The interaction leads to a rapid energy transfer process from NaYF4:Yb,Tm crystal to graphene sheets in His/OA-GQD, producing the fluorescence quenching. Fig. 4C also shows that the combination of GY with GQD brings a red shift of fluorescence spectrum of DOX. This is because the combination of His/OA-GQD with DOX enlarges the electron delocalization of DOX and results in the red shift of fluorescence spectrum. To further understand the interaction between GY and DOX, the isothermal titration calorimetry measurement was carried out. Fig. 4D presents the thermodynamic parameters of binding process. The Gibbs free energy change (ΔG) is lower than zero, verifying that the binding is a spontaneous process. Both the entropy change (ΔS) and enthalpy change (ΔH) are negative. This demonstrates the existence of Van der Waals’ force or hydrogen bond between the GY and the DOX [42–45]. The multiple bindings between DOX and GY produces strong interaction and enhances the stability of GY-DOX nanoconjugates. This greatly helps to reduce the toxic and side effects to normal cells caused by the premature drug leakage during the drug delivery.

The DOX loading capacity of CGYAu system was examined by monitoring the change in the DOX loading content with the incubation time. The time-dependent DOX loading content profile of CGYAu-DOX system is presented at Fig. 4E. An ultrafast adsorption behavior is observed within 5 min from Fig. 4E. The loading content (LC) of DOX reaches to 410% within 5 min. The LC value gently increases until the incubation time is about 5 min. Within 30 min incubation time, the maximum LC value reaches to 461.2% with the encapsulation efficiency (EE) of 99.28%. Such an ultrahigh DOX loading content is mainly due to large surface of the His/OA-GQD and strong interaction between the DOX and the His/OA-GQD.

The drug release performance of CGYAu-DOX system was investigated at different pH values (pH 5.0, 6.0 or 7.4) and with and without the NIR laser radiation (Fig. 4F). The CGYAu-DOX system exhibits both pH and NIR light-induced DOX release. In a pH 7.4 Tris-HCI buffer condition (a similar pH in cell cytosol) without the NIR laser radiation, DOX slowly releases from the CGYAu-DOX. The release rate is less than 7.7% within 60 min, verifying that the CGYAu-DOX offers a high stability in the environment of cell cytosol. The high stability effectively avoids the premature drug leakage during the drug delivery. However, the release in an acidic environment (pH 5.0, a similar pH in the environment of intracellular vesicles) is much higher than that in a near-neutral condition (pH 7.4). The release rate of DOX after 60 min reaches to 25.2% at pH of 5.0 without the NIR laser radiation. The pH-dependent releasing is due to the change in hydrogen-bond between DOX and GY at different pH conditions. At pH 7.4, GY can bind to DOX via hydrogen bond and leads to a strong interaction between DOX and GY, indicating a low release rate of DOX from the CGYAu-DOX. At pH 5.0, the part of hydrogen bonds is destroyed, because of the protonation of –NH2 and results in increasing the release rate of DOX [45]. Further, the release performance triggered by the NIR laser radiation was examined at pH value of 5.0, 6.0 or 7.4 under the NIR laser radiation. It was found that the NIR laser radiation results in an obviously increase in the release rate of DOX for all pH conditions (Fig. 4F). The increase in DOX release rate reaches to 65.2% for pH 5.0, 71.5% for pH 6.0 and 74.1% for pH 7.4. Due to high efficiency of upconversion emission of GY, CGYAu displays excellent photothermal effect. The hyperthermia caused by the photothermal effect of Au destroys the cell membranes outer shell of CGYAu-DOX and enhance the drug diffusion from pores of GY into the surrounding medium. These accelerate the release of DOX from CGYAu-DOX system [41]. The release rate of CGYAu-DOX system reaches to 95.2% at pH 5.0 with NIR laser radiation within 60 min. The high drug release of DOX greatly helps to improve the efficacy of chemotherapy during the cancer treatment.

The intracellular drug release behavior was tested by confocal imaging of MGC-803 cancer cells incubated in free DOX or other nanocarriers after 4 h with and without the NIR laser radiation. The red fluorescence of DOX is so weak that it is difficult to be observed in the confocal image of the cells treated with CGYAu or free DOX (shown in Fig. 5 A). This confirms that the internalization of DOX was almost restrained when the cells were treated with free DOX in the absence nanocarrier. However, the red fluorescence signal was found in the nucleus if the cells were incubated in the CGYAu-DOX for 4 h, indicating the endocytosis of CGYAu-DOX. As the cell membrane proteins keep homotypic targeting ability, the existence of MGC-803 cell membranes enhances the cellular uptake of GYAu-DOX [45]. Stimulated by acidic environment in the inner of cancer cells (pH < 5), the CGYAu-DOX entered into the inner of cancer cells rapidly releases the DOX molecules from the CGYAu-DOX. The released DOX was delivered from the cytoplasm into the nuclei, indicating a strong red fluorescence of DOX in the nucleus. Under the NIR laser radiation, the red fluorescence signal was largely enhanced. This verifies that the NIR laser radiation accelerates the release of DOX. Due to high efficiency of upconversion luminescence, the NIR laser radiation induces a significant photothermal effect. The hyperthermia caused by the photothermal effect rapidly increases the temperature of CGYAu-DOX and accelerates the release of DOX from CGYAu-DOX. In addition, the hyperthermia destroys the cell membrane coating on the GYAu-DOX surface. This improves the permeability and fluidity of MGC-cell membranes. The above factors remarkably improve the cellular internalization of CGYAu-DOX system as well as subsequent release and delivery of DOX in the cells, finally resulting in an obviously enhanced red fluorescence signal in the nucleus [46]. To quantifically evaluate the intracellular uptake of different systems, the flow cytometry assay was used for investigating the intracellular drug release performance of CGYAu-DOX. In comparison to the cells treated by free DOX, the cells incubated in the GYAu-DOX realize a faster release of DOX and indicates a slight increased red fluorescence signal (shown in Fig. 5B). This is because the combination of GY with DOX brings a better amphiphilic structure. The high surface activity makes the GYAu-DOX easier to enter into the inner of cells compared with free DOX molecules. GYAu-DOX in the cells releases DOX from the GYAu-DOX and results in an increased red fluorescence signal. In comparison to the cells treated with the GYAu-DOX, the cells incubated with the CGYAu-DOX indicates a faster increase in the red fluorescence signal. After the incubated in the CGYAu-DOX for 2 h, the red fluorescence signal is more than 2.2-fold that of the cells treated with the GYAu-DOX. The fact verifies that the coating of MGC-803 cell membranes towards GYAu-DOX plays an important role to improving the cellular uptake of CGYAu-DOX. Under the NIR laser radiation, the red fluorescence signal rapidly increases. At the incubation of 2 h, the red fluorescence signal is more than 2.3-fold that without the NIR laser radiation. The red fluorescence signal increases with the prolongation of incubation time. The above results demonstrate that the CGYAu can serve as an ideal drug delivery system of cancer with both pH and light-triggered drug release.

The antiproliferatie effect of CGYAu, DOX, GYAu-DOX and CGYAu-DOX to MGC-803 cells was tested by using sulforhodamine B assay. Fig. 6
shows that the CGYAu gives almost no any dark cytotoxicity against MGC-803 cells, verifying that the CGYAu as the DOX nanocarrier offers good biocompatibility. However, the CGYAu indicates an obviously increased cytotoxicity under the NIR laser radiation. Its IC50 value was found to be 2.8 μg mL−1. This is due to the hyperthermia caused by the photothermal effect of Au under the NIR laser radiation. On the one hand, the coating of MGC-803 improves the cellular uptake of CGYAu, leading to an increased amounts of CGYAu in the inner of the cells. On the other hand, GY in the CGYAu can effectively convert the NIR exciting light into the ultraviolet and visible emission due to its high efficiency of upconversion luminescence. These strong emission lights can be rapidly absorbed by Au to produce more heat, leading to a higher phototoxicity towards the cells. The viability of cells treated with free DOX has no obvious change after the NIR laser radiated. The result confirms that the NIR laser radiation does not affect the cytotoxicity of free DOX. The IC50 value of the cells treated with the CGYAu-DOX is 0.98 μg mL−1. The value is lower than 3.2-fold that of the GYAu-DOX, indicating a much stronger cytotoxicity. This is because the coating of MGC-803 cell membrane proteins remarkably enhances the cellular uptake of CGYAu-DOX. The strongest cytotoxicity occurs in the cells incubated in the CGYAu-DOX with the NIR laser irradiation. Its IC50 value is lower than 4.9-fold that of the cells treated with the CGYAu-DOX in dark. This is because the NIR laser radiation accelerates the release of DOX from the CGYAu-DOX and induces the phototoxicity of Au. Upon the NIR laser irradiation, almost all cells incubated in the CGYAu-DOX were destroyed to death. These results demonstrate that the synergy of chemo/photothermal therapy produces an obviously enhanced antiproliferatie effect.

The biodistribution of CGYAu-DOX was investigated in the MGC-803 orthotopic gastric tumor model (Fig. 7). In the DOX group, the accumulation of DOX in the heart, lung or kidney is obviously faster that in the tumor. A high concentration of DOX was quickly detected in the heart, lung and kidney, which is obviously higher than that in the tumor. The biodistribution characteristic of DOX leads to the toxicity to normal tissues. In the CGYAu-DOX group, the accumulation of DOX in the tumors is obviously faster than that in the heart tissues with and without the NIR irradiation. At the first 1 h, the concentration of DOX in the tumor reaches to 2.7-fold that in the heart tissues. In the next time from 4 to 24 h, the CGYAu-DOX still displays a high accumulation in the tumors. The high accumulation of DOX is attributed to the MGC-803 cell membranes-mediated homotypic targeting potential and the enhanced the permeability and retention effect. Under the NIR laser radiation, the accumulation of DOX in the tumor was remarkably increased after the injection for 4 h, which is more than 2.1-fold that without NIR laser radiation. The results prove that the NIR laser radiation can accelerate the release of DOX from the CGYAu-DOX and bring a higher accumulation of DOX in the tumor tissues. This is beneficial to improve the efficacy and reduce the toxic and side effect to normal cells during the cancer therapy.

The therapeutic effects of CGYAu-DOX nanoconjugate upon drug release in the tumor-bearing mouse were evaluated. The mouse were divided into saline, CGYAu, CGYAu with NIR laser radiation, DOX, CGYAu-DOX and CGYAu-DOX with the NIR laser radiation groups. In a typical experiment, the nanoconjugates were intratumorally injected into the tumor sites with total drug dose of 2.0 mg kg−1. The tumor volume of mouse was monitored after the injection. Fig. 8 A and B show that the mice treated with the CGYAu exhibits a similar growth tread as the saline group, verifying no antitumor activity. Interestingly, a significant antitumor growth efficiency was observed in the CGYAu with the NIR laser radiation group. The photothermal effect of Au in the CGYAu under the NIR laser radiation leads to an obvious temperature increase in the interior of tumor cells, producing a distinct phototherapeutic effect. After treated with the CGYAu-DOX without the NIR laser radiation, the tumor volume of mice reduced by 56.3%. The high antitumor efficacy is caused by pH-controlled release of DOX from the CGYAu-DOX. The acidity in the cancer cells is stronger than that in the normal cells, thus the CGYAu-DOX entered the inner of cancer cells will partly releases DOX from the CGYAu-DOX and leads to an obviously enhanced antitumor efficacy. The tumor volume was dramatically reduced to 0.4% at the next 20 days after the intravenous injection of CGYAu-DOX. Meanwhile, the systemic toxicity of nanoconjugates was also tested via observing the body weight changes after the injection. Only mice treated with free DOX displays an obvious loss process of the body weight after the injection among all groups, indicating that all nanoconjugates studied absence severe systemic toxicity to mice. For the CGYAu-DOX with NIR laser radiation, its excellent therapeutic efficacy is attributed to a significant synergy between chemotherapy and photothermal therapy.

The in vivo metabolism and degradation of CGYAu-DOX were studied by monitoring the Y element distribution in different organs of mice with the NIR radiation after the CGYAu-DOX intravenous injection. Fig. 9 presents the Y concentration in the organs of mice detected by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry. It can be seen from Fig. 9 that a relatively high level of Y element appears in the tumor, lung, liver and spleen within the first 8 h. This proves that the CGYAu-DOX was accumulated through the reticuloendothelial system absorption. With prolonging the time, the Y concentration slowly decreases in all organs. In addition, the Y concentration in feces or urine realizes the maximum value at the first 8 h. Continue to prolong the time, the Y concentration slowly reduces. The above results also prove that CGYAu-DOX was accumulated and further metabolized by way of feces and urine.

In vivo toxicity of the GY was tested via monitoring the change in blood biochemistry and liver and kidney function markers after the injection of GY. All changes caused by the injection are within normal ranges to the healthy mice (Fig. 10 ). The H&E analysis of major organs including heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney further reveals no obvious damage in the CGYAu-DOX group. The result indicates that the GY offers an acceptable biosafety used in drug delivery and cancer theranostics (shown in Fig. 8C).

One concept for fabrication of His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocage has been demonstrated. Due to unique structures and properties, hybrid of His/OA-GQD and NaYF4:Yb,Tm achieves to 140.2-fold enhancement of upconversion fluorescence, excellent stability in a physiological buffer condition and ultrahigh DOX loading content of 461.2% at nearly 100% of encapsulation efficiency. The strong upconversion photoluminscence of His/OA-GQD-NaYF4:Yb,Tm nanocages remarkably improves the photothermal effect of gold nanoparticles. This helps to accelerate the NIR laser-triggered drug release and produce a significant photothermal therapy of tumor. The drug delivery system, composing of the hybrid and gold nanoparticle as the core and MGC-803 cell membrane as the shell, realizes selective targeting of homotypic tumor cells, both pH and light-stimulated DOX release, and chemo/photothermal therapy with a much higher anticanceractivity compared with free DOX. In vitro and in vivo therapeutic evaluations confirm the high anticancer activity and excellent biocompatibility. The study also open up one new way to design and synthesis of nanocarriers for cancer’s early diagnosis and in situ treatment.
